text: Hi, I am Dr. Shinjini Pandey and today we will be discussing a case of a large fibroid uterus which was managed by myomectomy and just briefing you with the patient details. So this patient is a thirty five year old lady. She was married for about one year or so and came to me with the complaint of mass per abdomen. She was also keen on a pregnancy and on examination the uterus was around twenty eight weeks in size. She did not have any other history of either menorrhagia or dysmenorrhea.

On a routine workup on ultrasound as well as confirmation on MRI, it was found to be a large fibroid of around almost nineteen centimeters on long axis occupying the anterior uterine wall the lower anterior uterine wall and extending into the broad ligament.

So in this surgery basically we will discuss on how to perform a large myomectomy at oddly located areas like for instance in this case the lower anterior uterine wall extending into the left broad ligament, how do we manage hemostasis, what are the steps that we take in order to avoid bleeding and also what are the steps which we will take to kind of expedite the surgery because as we all know that very large fibroids may take a long time.
So let us start the video now.

The initial view reveals the left round ligament stretched due to the presence of a large subserosal fibroid. The corneal structures on the left are displaced towards the right side of the pelvis. The uterine fundus is obscured by the fibroid and deviated to the right pelvic brim. The right adnexa is challenging to locate, but the fallopian tube is visible upon manipulation of the uterus, indicating the fundus has shifted to the right.

The surgeon begins the myomectomy by dividing the round ligament and making a liberal incision into the anterior peritoneum to shift the bladder down. Using a laparoscopic sharer, the peritoneal incision is extended backwards towards the iliac vessels, ensuring adequate hemostasis.

The surgeon uses a Babcock clamp to hold the cut edge of the peritoneum, revealing the external iliac vessels beneath. Silvery white fibers running transversely are released, while the surgeon focuses on locating the ureter and the uterine artery at its origin.

The surgeon has shifted the bladder completely down and is attempting to develop space at the lateral pelvic side wall. The large fibroid is visibly obstructing the operating space, making it challenging to maneuver. No myoma spiral or traction has been applied to the uterus yet. Four working ports are visible, with two on the left side used by the surgeon and two by the assistant surgeon.

The surgeon shifts the peritoneal cut to the contralateral side and moves the bladder fold. Diluted vasopressin is injected in a small dose at the site intended for myoma spiral insertion. The myoma spiral is then placed after completing a certain amount of dissection.

The surgeon inserts a myoma spiral into the large, vascular fibroid, creating traction that improves operating space. Large veins are visible on the fibroid's surface, and the obliterated umbilical artery is traced backwards.

The surgeon makes small movements to prevent bleeding while locating the ureter. Once visible, the obliterated umbilical artery is traced backwards to reveal the uterine artery at its origin. Clips are applied to ligate the left uterine artery, highlighting its superior and inferior margins.

The surgeon installs diluted vasopressin at the site of the intended myoma screw insertion. The myoma screw is then shifted to the left-sided upper port, providing traction from the left upper side. The surgeon assesses the need to shift the bladder further down due to the low origin of the fibroid, which may risk encountering the bladder during enucleation.

The surgeon divides the right-sided round ligament and proceeds to take the right-sided uterine artery. The myoma spiral is frequently shifted due to the large fibroid, with small doses of vasopressin used to minimize unexpected bleeding at the insertion site.

The surgeon opens the pelvic triangle and locates the obliterated umbilical artery, tracing it backwards to identify the uterine artery at its origin. The uterine artery is visible, and a second clip is applied for ligation. The ureter is located, and the surgeon moves laterally to find the iliac vessels. The vasopressin is re-injected in larger amounts to facilitate aqua dissection.

The surgeon begins the incision on the fibroid using cold scissors, carefully cutting through the tissue. The preference for cold scissors is due to their potential for better scar healing, although an energy source may be used if blanching is insufficient or if bleeding occurs.

The surgeon manages smaller fibroids by making incisions with scissors, but for larger fibroids, especially near the angle or extreme end, an energy source is sometimes used to cut the pseudocapsule. A liberal incision is given to facilitate the fibroid popping out, reducing the effort and time required for enucleation in the laparoscopic procedure.

The surgeon encounters a large fibroid with significant degeneration, making it challenging to identify the correct plane between the pseudo capsule and the fibroid. The surgeon cuts deeper into the fibroid substance, revealing the fibroid's distinct appearance as it is dissected and begins to protrude.

The surgeon identifies the correct plane for enucleation as the pseudo capsule thins near the broad ligament. The fibroid appears soft and degenerated, requiring strategic shifting of the myoma spiral and use of multiple instruments to facilitate enucleation from all sides.

The surgeon uses strong holding graspers, such as a needle holder, to stabilize the myometrial site during enucleation of a large fibroid. The assistant aids in the procedure, revealing the fibroid's large mass. Oblique fibers of the pseudo capsule are visible, trapping the fibroid, and are carefully cut flush to the fibroid. At the base, there is potential for bleeding, requiring readiness with a bipolar or vessel sealer to avoid opening the cavity.

Now, another word here is opening the cavity in my opinion is not a disaster if it opens it opens you only have to take care while suturing and contrary to what others may believe i do feel that if it opens it is a natural drain that has been put the seepage of blood Outside is much easier and so collection or hematoma formation is really unlikely in cases where the cavity opens. All of us know that hematoma formation can lead to a poor scar. In fact, when I attempt very, very large fibroids, which have a deep intramural or even a submucosal component, sometimes I will deliberately perforate the cavity to let out the bleeding.

The fibroid is in the final stages of enucleation, with visible myohypoplasia indicating its long-standing presence. The smooth border and curvature of the fibroid are apparent, guiding the surgeon in completing the enucleation. Blood begins to seep up as the fibroid is about to be removed, followed by the excision of a sub-serosal fibroid and the commencement of suturing.

The surgeon places an under-running suture at the deep base of the fibroid using number one o vicryl to address oozing from the bottom of the bed. A box suture is then applied, passing the needle through the base from one side, reversing direction, and exiting through both anterior and posterior flaps near the initial entry site, providing a tamponading effect to control bleeding. The anterior and posterior flaps are reassessed, and additional sutures are placed to achieve hemostasis in the vascular area near the broad ligament.

The surgeon closes the anterior and posterior flaps together in two layers using a barbed suture. The thinned-out flappy part is resected for a more compact closure. The surgeon traces the ureter to ensure it is safe and away from the line of closure.

The surgeon carefully traces the ureter to ensure it is not caught in the sutures, avoiding potential kinking or damage. The anterior flap is trimmed to create a regular edge, and a barbed suture is used to secure the tissue.

The surgeon uses round barbed sutures to close the large defect created by the myomectomy, applying two sutures from right to left and left to right. The ends are tied for additional security, although not necessary. The incision is compactly closed, ensuring the bladder is safely out of the surgical field. A figure-of-eight suture is placed to address any boggy areas below the suture line, providing additional tamponade.
The surgeon removes the clip and reconstructs the round ligaments, followed by closing the peritoneal defect to prevent bowel loops from slipping in, achieving a neat final closure.

The surgeon closes the left side similarly to the right, reconstructing the anatomy. A boggy layer of the posterior peritoneum is identified, revealing the fibroid beneath. The surgeon opens this area to drain and ensure there is no bleeding or fluid collection.

The surgeon inspects the loose hanging stained peritoneum, ensuring there is no hematoma or collection. The fibroid bed appears loose but without any collection. An adhesion barrier is placed over the area to promote better healing.
